region_of_origin: China (Fujian / Guangdong); served in Singapore via Starbucks
food_subgroup: Brewed tea, unsweetened, no added sugar

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard tall cup of brewed oolong tea, approximately 355 ml, as typically served at Starbucks Singapore with no added sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, oolong, brewed — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 14352)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for brewed oolong tea (no sugar added) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent plain brewed oolong tea per 100 g of beverage. This aligns with the Starbucks Singapore product which is HPB-endorsed as no added sugar.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of beverages — plain brewed tea analogue; University of Sydney GI Database convention for non-glycemic beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed oolong tea contains negligible carbohydrates (approximately 0.15 g per 100 g) and no added sugar. No direct GI test for oolong tea is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. By established convention for non-glycemic beverages (plain tea, black coffee, water), GI is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) and gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per 355 ml serving is 0.0 due to effectively zero glycemic carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (3 mg per 100 g); no added salt or sugar
hypertension.guidance: Plain oolong tea with no added sugar is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, as its sodium content is negligible (approximately 11 mg per 355 ml serving) and it contains no added salt, sugar, or cream.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and no added sugar; HPB-endorsed no added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Oolong tea with no added sugar has a glycemic load of effectively zero per serving and is endorsed by the Health Promotion Board Singapore under its Healthier Choice Symbol for no added sugar, making it a safe beverage choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol, zero saturated fat; contains polyphenols with antioxidant properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain oolong tea contains no fat, no cholesterol, and no saturated fat, and its polyphenol content (including theaflavins and catechins) has been associated in observational studies with modest cardiovascular protective effects, making it a heart-friendly beverage choice.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Zen Clouds Oolong Tea is a brewed oolong tea beverage served at Starbucks Singapore (One Fullerton location) with no added sugar, endorsed by the Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore under its Healthier Choice Symbol programme. Oolong tea (乌龙茶, Wū Lóng Chá) is a traditional Chinese tea originating from Fujian and Guangdong provinces in China. It is a partially oxidised tea, positioned between green tea and black tea in terms of oxidation level (typically 15–85%), which gives it a distinctive floral, fruity, and slightly roasted character. The Zen Clouds variant emphasises fresh floral aromatic notes. Nutritionally, plain brewed oolong tea is essentially a zero-calorie beverage: per 100 g it provides approximately 1 kcal, 0 g protein, 0 g fat, 0.15 g carbohydrate, and 0 g sugar. It contains trace minerals including potassium (12 mg/100 g) and sodium (3 mg/100 g). Oolong tea is naturally rich in polyphenols, including catechins, theaflavins, and thearubigins, which have antioxidant properties. It also contains caffeine (approximately 16–37 mg per 100 ml of brewed tea). Clinically, oolong tea with no added sugar is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its negligible glycemic load, zero fat, and very low sodium content. It is vegan, vegetarian, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free. The beverage is typically served hot or iced in a tall cup (approximately 355 ml) at Starbucks Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is oolong tea good for diabetes?
Is oolong tea suitable for weight loss?
Oolong tea vs green tea nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 14352) for brewed oolong tea, which is an exact match to the product category. The product is confirmed as no added sugar by HPB Singapore endorsement. GI is set by convention for non-glycemic beverages per University of Sydney GI Database framework.
notes: 1. energy_kcal is 1.0 kcal per 100 g — this is the USDA-reported value for plain brewed oolong tea; the beverage is effectively a near-zero-calorie drink. 2. protein_g is naturally zero as plain brewed oolong tea contains no protein by nature. 3. fat_g is naturally zero as plain brewed oolong tea contains no fat by nature. 4. sugar_g is confirmed zero — the product is HPB-endorsed as no added sugar and plain brewed oolong tea contains no free sugars. 5. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero for brewed tea liquid (fibre remains in the leaf). 6. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention for non-glycemic beverages per University of Sydney GI Database framework. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for brewed oolong tea. 8. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 9. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg are reported as 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy for brewed oolong tea — confirmed zero or below detection threshold (trace). 10. The food_name is normalised to the canonical English name 'Oolong Tea'; the commercial product name 'Zen Clouds Oolong Tea' is preserved in aliases. 11. Cuisine field is set to 'Chinese' reflecting the origin of oolong tea; the input cuisine tag 'Coffee' reflects the Starbucks restaurant category on field survey and is not the food's actual cuisine classification. 12. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as this is a beverage served in Singapore. Japanese alias also included as oolong tea is widely consumed in Japan. 13. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301103 and SNOMED CT code 227407002 are used as best available matches; FoodEx2 code A00LC is used for oolong tea infusion. Reviewers should verify these codes against current ontology releases.
